range.nativeRange: Mediterranean region and Middle East; naturalized widely in temperate regions

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US; cultivated throughout the US; not naturalized in the wild

zone.description: 2–10 (grown as annual in Zone 6a); cool-season crop

description.height: 18–24 in in first year (vegetative); up to 3–4 ft when bolting in second year
description.spacing: 10–12 in apart, in rows 18–24 in apart
description.growthRate: Slow to moderate; slow to establish from seed
description.timeToMaturity: 100–130 days from transplant to harvest; one of the longest-season vegetables
description.lifespan: Biennial grown as annual; bolts and flowers in year two

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun preferred; tolerates light afternoon shade, which can help in hot summers
lightAndWater.water: Very high — consistently moist soil required throughout the season; does not tolerate drying out; drought causes stringy, bitter stalks

drainagePreference.description: Moisture-retentive but not waterlogged; consistent moisture is more important than drainage here

claySoilPerformance.description: Moderate; celery needs consistent moisture which clay provides, but clay compaction inhibits the oxygen exchange celery roots need; well-amended clay with compost is workable; raised beds with moisture-retentive but well-structured mix are superior for celery production in heavy-soil gardens

soil.texturePreference: Rich, loamy, well-amended soil with high organic matter; heavy clay must be amended heavily with compost; poor soil produces poor stalks
soil.rootCharacter: Shallow fibrous roots; does not compete well — needs loose, uncompacted soil

bloom.bloomTime: Year two (June–August) if allowed to overwinter; as annual, bolts in midsummer heat
bloom.bloomDescription: Tiny white flowers in compound umbels (flat-topped clusters); classic Apiaceae form
bloom.pollination: Insect-pollinated (bees and other small pollinators); highly attractive to beneficial insects when in flower

yield.edibleParts: Stalks (petioles), leaves, seeds; all parts edible — leaves used as herb/seasoning; stalks eaten fresh or cooked; seeds used as spice
yield.harvestSeason: Late summer to fall (August–October in Zone 6a); harvest outer stalks progressively or cut whole plant at base
yield.medicinalUses: Traditional use as diuretic and digestive aid; seeds used in herbal medicine; anti-inflammatory properties documented; not a primary medicinal crop
yield.craftOtherYield: Seeds harvested for spice or next-year seed saving (allow one plant to bolt)

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Furanocoumarins in leaves and stems can cause phototoxic skin reactions (photodermatitis) when handling plant in sunlight — wear gloves if processing large quantities; generally safe to eat
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Generally non-toxic to dogs and cats in normal food quantities; large amounts may cause digestive upset; seeds not recommended for pets

roles: Food crop (stalks, leaves, seeds); insectary plant when flowering (umbels attract parasitic wasps and beneficial flies); short-term ground cover in vegetable beds
companionPlants: nasturtium; cabbage family plants benefit from proximity
antagonists: Parsnip and carrots (same Apiaceae family — can share aphid and carrot fly pest pressure; avoid planting in rotation after other Apiaceae); chard may inhibit germination

wildlife.attracts: Parasitic wasps, hoverflies, small bees, and other beneficial insects when in flower — excellent insectary value; flowers in year two only
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Black swallowtail butterfly (Papilio polyxenes) larval host — one of the primary hosts along with dill, parsley, and parsnip

deer.description: Low resistance — deer will browse celery readily

salt.description: Low — sensitive to road salt and saline soils

drought.description: Very low — one of the least drought-tolerant vegetables; wilts quickly and produces poor-quality stalks under any water stress

tolerances.other: Poor heat tolerance — bolts when temperatures consistently exceed 80°F; best harvested before midsummer heat peaks or grown as a fall crop

maintenance.maintenanceLevel: High — one of the most demanding vegetables to grow; requires consistent moisture, long indoor seed start, careful transplanting, and often blanching
maintenance.pruningNotes: Blanching: mound soil around stalks, wrap with cardboard or pipe/tube, or exclude light for 2–3 weeks before harvest to produce pale, mild-flavored stalks; self-blanching cultivars reduce this labor. Remove any bolting flower stalks if trying to extend harvest
propagationMethods: Seed — surface sow or barely cover (light aids germination); start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost (early February for Zone 6a); seeds are tiny and slow to germinate (14–21 days); transplant after last frost when nights stay above 50°F; direct sowing not practical for long-season types

cultivarNotes:
Stalk celery (var. dulce) cultivar types vary by climate region. In cooler zones (Zone 5–6), early-maturing and self-blanching types are preferred due to the shorter growing window. In warmer zones (Zone 7–9), longer-season types can be used for fall crops. Self-blanching types reduce labor across all climates.
- Tango: Compact, self-blanching, reliable for shorter seasons; good flavor without blanching effort; well-suited to Zone 5–7
- Conquistador: Early maturing (~80 days), performs well in variable conditions; good choice where season is marginal; reliable across Zone 5–7
- Utah 52-70 Improved: Classic heirloom; good flavor; does benefit from blanching for mildness; well-adapted to eastern US; performs best Zone 6–8
- Tall Utah: Traditional standard for stalk celery; reliable but requires longer season and blanching attention; better suited Zone 7–9 or long-season Zone 6 with fall timing
Cutting/leaf celery (var. secalinum) — strongly recommended as a low-maintenance alternative for most US home gardeners:
- Par-Cel (or "Soup Celery"): Leaf/cutting celery type; far more heat-tolerant, drought-tolerant, and forgiving than stalk types; grown for leaves and stems used as an herb; no blanching required; much easier to grow across a wide range of US climates; recommended for most home gardeners
Note: Celeriac (var. rapaceum — see separate profile Apium graveolens rapaceum.md) is generally a more practical root crop alternative for permaculture settings; less water-demanding per unit of yield.

suggestedUse: Annual vegetable in raised bed or kitchen garden; leaf/cutting celery types work in herb strip plantings; stalk types require dedicated bed space with reliable irrigation
gardenUse.designPlacement: Vegetable garden, raised bed, cool-season crop requiring consistent moisture
gardenUse.acquisitionStatus: Not yet acquired; seed widely available from mainstream and heirloom seed suppliers (Johnny's Selected Seeds, Fedco, Baker Creek)

pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins: raised beds recommended; consistent moisture retention is the key need; compost-rich raised bed mix suits celery well
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Raised beds with compost-rich mix; consistent moisture is the key cultural requirement
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: 'Tango', 'Conquistador', or 'Utah 52-70R' are reliable for Zone 6a; start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost; harvest before hard freeze; the short growing window at Zone 6a makes early-maturing types preferable
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Raised beds; start indoors in February; Zone 6a timing requires early-maturing cultivars; needs consistent irrigation through summer; celeriac may be a more practical alternative for most growers at this site
pennsylvaniaHegins.siteObservations: The red clay soil at Hegins is a significant challenge — would need heavy compost amendment and reliable irrigation. Leaf/cutting celery (Par-Cel type) is the more realistic choice for this site. Full stalk celery would require raised bed with irrigation access.
